Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'd dedicate about 32 hours a week to studying, but it wasn't spread evenly across the week. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd spend a lot of time going through textbooks and creating my own written summaries. Most of my deep studying happened outside the LMS, using printed materials and notes I'd compiled. I found that writing things out by hand helped me retain information much better than clicking through online modules.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied quite a bit. I didn't stick to a rigid schedule because I've learned that I work best when I follow my energy levels rather than a clock. Some weeks I'd study intensively for several days straight, then take breaks. Deadlines weren't really my main driver since I focused more on truly understanding the material. My extracurricular activities also meant I had to be flexible with my timing.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check if there were any announcements or new materials posted. Sometimes I'd pop in to see discussion posts from peers or to download resources I needed. I wasn't logging in constantly though—maybe just when I needed something specific. The LMS was more of a resource hub for me rather than my primary learning space.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, especially early in the semester to gather all the materials I needed. But once I had downloaded the readings and understood the course structure, I didn't feel the need to be on there constantly. My routine was built more around my physical notes and textbooks.
Q5: The reading materials and any downloadable PDFs were gold for me. Since I learn best through reading and writing, having access to comprehensive written content was essential. I also found the discussion posts useful for getting different perspectives on complex topics. The peer interactions helped me see things from angles I hadn't considered, even if I didn't post constantly myself.
Q6: I tended to skip over a lot of the video content, honestly. Watching videos just isn't how I learn best—I'd rather read the same information in half the time. Some of the interactive activities felt a bit redundant when I'd already grasped the concept through reading. I also didn't complete many of the smaller tasks because I was focused on the bigger picture of understanding the core material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it genuinely deepened my understanding or challenged my thinking. I wasn't interested in busywork or things that just checked boxes. If I could see how something connected to exam content or real-world applications, I was all in. Quality over quantity has always been my approach.
Q8: Absolutely they felt different. Required activities sometimes felt like hoops to jump through, even when they weren't particularly useful for my learning style. Optional activities I could evaluate on their own merit—if they looked valuable, I'd engage; if not, I'd skip them guilt-free. I appreciated having that autonomy to direct my own learning.
Q9: Oh, constantly. The majority of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and print them out so I could annotate them by hand. I used external textbooks heavily and created my own comprehensive notes. I also took eight online courses, so I was pulling knowledge from multiple sources beyond just this course's LMS. My desk was always covered in handwritten summaries and highlighted printouts.
Q10: Most of my studying, actually. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes, re-reading printed materials, and synthesizing information across different sources. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also discuss concepts with peers in person or via messaging apps, working through problems together. My best learning moments happened completely offline.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then work with them extensively offline. A single PDF download might represent ten hours of studying on my end. I preferred having physical copies I could spread out and compare side by side. The LMS was really just the starting point for accessing content.
Q12: There were some weeks where my extracurricular commitments got intense and I had to prioritize. But honestly, I never found it that hard to stay engaged with the actual learning—I just engaged differently than the system might expect. My attendance wasn't great, but that's because I found independent study more effective for my learning style. I stayed connected to the material even when I wasn't physically present.
Q13: Managing multiple online courses definitely affected how I allocated my time on any single LMS. I had to be strategic about where I focused my energy. Sometimes I'd batch my work, spending concentrated time on one course before switching to another. Fatigue was rarely an issue since I kept my stress levels pretty low through mindfulness practices and good time management.
Q14: Occasionally I'd find myself scrolling through the LMS without really absorbing anything—just going through the motions to see what was there. Those moments usually happened when I was tired or distracted. But I recognized when that was happening and would step away rather than waste time on surface-level engagement. I'd come back later when I could focus properly.
Q15: I'll admit I postponed some of the smaller assignments, which is why my completion rate looks low. It wasn't avoidance exactly—I was making calculated decisions about where to invest my time. I prioritized deep understanding over completing every single task. Looking at my exam scores, that trade-off worked out well for me academically.
Q16: Deadlines influenced me less than they might influence other students. I wasn't scrambling at the last minute because I'd been consistently studying the material all along, just not through the LMS. When deadlines approached for major assessments, I'd make sure I was prepared, but my preparation happened mostly offline. The LMS was where I'd submit, not where I'd prepare.
Q17: Feedback was helpful when I received it, though I didn't depend on it to stay motivated. I appreciated knowing where I stood, and good feedback confirmed I was on the right track with my independent study approach. If I got constructive criticism, I'd incorporate it into my notes and adjust my understanding. But I was pretty self-directed in my learning overall.
Q18: The course structure gave me the flexibility I needed to learn my own way. Having materials available for download was crucial. I appreciated that not everything required constant online presence. The structure allowed me to front-load my learning—gathering resources early and then working through them at my own pace throughout the semester.
Q19: The availability of downloadable written content made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when important information was only available in video format or required synchronous participation. My attendance was lower because I found I could learn the material more efficiently on my own. The discussion forums were nice but not essential to my process.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for resources, exploring different materials, or trying to find what works for them. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. They could also be dealing with confusion or trying to understand expectations. Or maybe, like me, they're gathering everything they need before doing their real work offline.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS activity looks pretty minimal, but I scored a 96.78 on exams and earned a Distinction. The system can't see my hours of reading, note-taking, and synthesis work. Some students are just efficient—we know what we need, we get it, and we do the real learning elsewhere. Low platform activity doesn't mean low engagement with learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and what they clicked on. They track submission times and forum participation. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement, and whether someone actually learned anything. They can't capture the hours I spent with printed readings or the depth of my understanding. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with ideas and working to understand them deeply. It's about curiosity and active thinking, not just showing up or clicking through content. For me, engagement looks like filling notebooks with summaries, making connections between concepts, and being able to apply what I've learned. It's internal and intellectual, not just behavioral.
Q24: Almost everything important about my engagement wouldn't show up. My extensive offline reading, my handwritten notes, my synthesis across multiple courses and sources—none of that is visible. The mindfulness practices that kept me balanced and focused don't register anywhere. My conversations with peers outside the platform, my genuine interest in the subject matter—the LMS just can't capture what learning actually looks like for me.
